questions.question: What is the return type of an `async` function that explicitly returns a `string`?
What does `Awaited<T>` do and how does it handle nested Promises?

choices.a: It gives the type `T` without any modification
choices.b: It recursively unwraps Promise-like types until the non-Promise value type is reached
choices.c: It only unwraps one level of Promise
choices.d: `Awaited<T>` is only used internally by TypeScript; developers should not use it directly

questions.explanation:
`Awaited<T>` (TypeScript 4.5+) recursively unwraps nested Promises:

```typescript
type A = Awaited<Promise<string>>;           // string
type B = Awaited<Promise<Promise<number>>>;  // number (two levels!)
type C = Awaited<string>;                    // string (not a promise)

// With ReturnType:
async function fetchData() { return { id: 1, name: 'Alice' }; }
type Data = Awaited<ReturnType<typeof fetchData>>;  // { id: number; name: string }
```

Before `Awaited<T>`, developers had to write manual conditional unwrapping. `Awaited<T>` also handles `PromiseLike` (thenable objects), not just actual `Promise` instances.

Essential when you have utility functions that deal with both sync and async values and need the resolved type.

questions.question: How do you type a function that handles `Promise.all` with different types per promise?

choices.b: `Promise.all` infers a tuple of Awaited types when given a tuple literal — use `as const` or a tuple type to preserve individual types
choices.c: TypeScript cannot infer mixed-type Promise.all results
choices.d: Use `Promise.allSettled` — it is the only typed version

questions.question: What pattern do you use to type a function that can return T synchronously or Promise<T> asynchronously?

choices.a: Always use `async` to ensure it is always async
choices.b: Use `T | Promise<T>` as the return type — callers must handle both cases or use `await` (which is a no-op on non-promises)
choices.c: TypeScript does not support mixed sync/async return types

questions.question: How should you type async error handling to make error paths visible in the type system?

choices.a: Use `try/catch` with typed catch blocks — catch variables can be typed as `Error`
choices.b: Use a Result type (`Ok<T> | Err<E>`) as the return type — errors become part of the type signature instead of invisible throws
choices.c: TypeScript's type system cannot model error paths at all
